name: David Fenyo
title: Professor
summary: In my research I apply mathematical, statistical and computational methods to provide a detailed understanding of the dynamics of cellular processes through the integration and visualization of data from multiple technologies—including mass spectrometry, next generation sequencing, and microscopy to provide a deeper understanding of biological systems.
keywords: proteomics; multi-omics integration; proteogenomics; machine learning; deep learning; computational pathology; multiplexed imaging; spatial omics; cancer biology; immune regulation

name: Jef D. Boeke
title: Professor
summary: Leads a synthetic genomics and genome writing program focused on designing, assembling, and installing very large DNA constructs to interrogate genome regulation and build faithful disease models.
keywords: Synthetic genomics; Genome writing; Genome engineering; Large DNA assembly; Landing pads; Cre RMCE; CRISPR Cas9; mSwAP In; YAV; Big DNA; Chromatin regulation; Functional genomics; Institute for Systems Genetics; Mammalian genome writing; Yeast homologous recombination; Assemblons; Big IN; Landing pad integration; Cre lox RMCE; CRISPR Cas9 genome editing; Mouse embryonic stem cells; Transgenic mouse engineering; Human locus humanization; TAF1; X linked dystonia parkinsonism; SVA retrotransposon elements; Transcription elongation roadblocks; HPRT1; Default genomic states; Synthetic yeast chromosomes; 3D genome organization; Chromatin profiling; RNA sequencing; ATAC sequencing; Hi C; CAGE sequencing; Automation and robotics for DNA assembly; Assemblatron; Jack In the Box vector system; SURE vector system

name: Jeffrey N. Weiser, MD
summary: The Weiser Lab investigates the molecular basis of host–pathogen interactions in the human respiratory tract, with a primary focus on Streptococcus pneumoniae colonization, transmission, and immune evasion.
keywords: Bacterial colonization and persistence in the upper respiratory tract; Host–pathogen interactions shaping innate and adaptive immunity; Streptococcus pneumoniae pathogenesis, carriage, and virulence regulation; Genetic and molecular determinants of bacterial fitness and transmission; Microbial competition, co-colonization, and population dynamics; Virus–bacterium co-infection, particularly influenza–pneumococcus interactions; Role of phosphorylcholine and other bacterial surface molecules in immune evasion; Mucosal immunity and age-dependent susceptibility to infection; Influence of the upper-airway microbiota on systemic immune responses; Bacteriology / Immunology; Bacteriology; Drivers of Microbial Pathogenesis; Immunology

name: Liam Holt
title: Associate Professor
summary: The Holt Lab studies how the physical state of the cell interior shapes information flow, with an emphasis on macromolecular crowding, intracellular pH, and biomolecular condensation.
keywords: Biomolecular condensates and phase separation as regulators of kinase signaling and phosphoregulation; Macromolecular crowding as a tunable cellular signal and determinant of reaction rates and diffusion; mTORC1 control of cytoplasmic biophysics through ribosome concentration and mesoscale crowding; Genetically encoded multimeric nanoparticles for high throughput microrheology and particle tracking in living cells; Carbon starvation and intracellular pH dynamics as signals driving transcriptional reprogramming in yeast; SWI SNF chromatin remodeling recruitment controlled by a pH sensitive low complexity domain in SNF5; Synthetic condensate design rules, scaffold flexibility, and client recruitment controlling signaling efficiency; Quantitative integration of imaging, biochemistry, and modeling to connect cell physics to gene regulation; Biophysics of signaling / biomolecular condensates / gene regulation under stress

name: Molly Gale Hammell
title: Associate Professor
summary: Molly Gale Hammell is an Associate Professor in the Institute for Systems Genetics at NYU Langone Health.

name: Paolo Mita
title: Research Assistant Professor
summary: Transposable elements (TE) are sequences of DNA that are capable of moving within a host genome, and thus are often referred to as “jumping genes.” DNA transposons use a “cut and paste” mechanism to move within a host genome while retrotransposons increase their amount using a “copy and paste” process.

name: Ran Brosh
title: Research Assistant Professor, Associate Director
summary: I have two roles: [1] as Faculty at ISG/Pathology I am leading the Dark Matter Project, a collaborative effort to use synthetic biology and genome writing to study the noncoding genome and its roles in health and disease.
keywords: ACE2 humanized mouse models; Big IN platform; CRISPR activation rescue; DHS element function mapping; SARS CoV 2 susceptibility models; SVA retrotransposon biology; Sox2 locus control region; TAF1 humanization; X linked dystonia parkinsonism; capture sequencing validation; chromatin and transcriptome profiling; landing pad based genome rewriting; large DNA payload integration; locus scale genome engineering; mSwAP In iterative rewriting; mammalian genome writing; synthetic regulatory genomics
